> When calling the super class, why require us to write super(Child, self) instead of just super()?

That's for compatibility reason only, in python3, you can write `super()` only. (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10482953/python-extending-with-using-super-python-3-vs-python-2)

My code contains a lot of this idea, because I need to consider python2's compatibility.

> In Java, all of a parent class's methods are automatically copied over to its children

This idea is as same as python. 

I believe It's an opinion-based thing. I prefer to write code in an explicit way. 
* If you are using parent function, we should tell others in the code. 
* If you are using class variable, we should tell others in the code.
* If the function is a staticmethod, we should not implement them as an instance method.
* If the function is a classmethod, we should not implement them as an instance method.
... (there are several examples)

**Python is a flexible language. Thus we have no need to follow any of them in above.** However, in my experience, while people trace code, writing them in an explicit way will enhance the readability. That's why I suggest you to modify in that way.

In modern python discussion, lots people are trying to promote [type hint](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/#type-definition-syntax) in conference. The reason is same, the language is flexible, but it can be solid too.

Can you end the sentence with a colon (`dependencies:`) here and everywhere else?  The ending colon is usually used to introduce a list, a definition, a quote, or an example.  

This would clearly indicate to the reader that the Python example that follows is part of this sentence, not the following one.  (In other words, that examples don't come before explanations.)  Sometimes it is  obvious, but other times it may not be, hence it is advantageous to have a unique style everywhere.

We use this style already in places like:
- http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation/installation-quick.html
- http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation/installation-detailed.html

Also in response to the previous comment.

I think it makes sense to segregate everything to do with Channels into the consumer. This way, the main manager code can be mostly about what the manager actually is, along with some additional utility functions (because we don't want the world to know about the consumer at all). The consumer is a specific implementation for outside infrastructure (Channels in this case) on which the manager sits, so it's sensible to make it handle everything to do with that infrastructure.

The manager itself used to have lots of test-specific functionality and that code wasn't in the tests either. Now some of it moved to the consumer, because it makes more sense there (and also the dispatcher is getting rather large, that's why I tried keeping as much Channels code out of it as possible).

As for the circle: it could be broken by moving all the low level Channels management code into the manager, but I think that would make everything much more ugly. The circle is controlled in that the manager only calls consumer methods which are call graph leaves. I could make those methods top-level instead of static.

However, if it can't reuse NDArrays internally in the graph executor there won't be any warnings, but rather it will just silently allocate new arrays. How about instead of mapping just 24 and 53, instead mapping based on ranges of values:
- `1-24` to `float32`
- `25-53` to `float64`
- `>=54` raises an exception.

This would follow the behavior of Postgres.
From https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.5/static/datatype-numeric.html#DATATYPE-NUMERIC-DECIMAL,
```
PostgreSQL also supports the SQL-standard notations float and float(p) for specifying inexact numeric types. Here, p specifies the minimum acceptable precision in binary digits. PostgreSQL accepts float(1) to float(24) as selecting the real type, while float(25) to float(53) select double precision. Values of p outside the allowed range draw an error. float with no precision specified is taken to mean double precision
```
